summary: In 1942, the President of the United States personally asked me to become Director of Extension during the war years. I had known him since around 1911 or 1915 and worked with him since 1915 or 1916, which made our collaboration smooth. We exchanged letters to align our views on how Extension should serve alumni and the public, especially focusing on lifelong learning. The President also appointed me to the National Advisory Committee to oversee wartime training programs, which was a challenging but important role.

California had one of the most adequate adult education and public service systems in the country by the early 1940s. The state’s public school system, junior colleges, and adult services reached over a million and a half people annually, almost free of charge. During the war, the College of Engineering at the University of California, Berkeley, took charge of important training courses to support wartime production. I worked closely with California institutions to develop Extension programs that served graduates and professionals across the state.

New York State was recognized as having the second most adequate general pattern of adult education services after California. I compared its system favorably alongside other states during my discussions about Extension programs. This context helped shape my recommendations to the President about how to serve alumni and professionals through Extension. While I was based in California, I was aware of New York’s strong adult education framework as a model.

Pennsylvania State University was one of the institutions noted for having good adult education services, alongside the University of Michigan. I mentioned Penn State when discussing how Extension programs should serve graduates and professionals to keep their knowledge current. Although my work was centered in California, I kept in mind the successful models from Penn State while advising the President. These examples helped me argue for a strong faculty-supported Extension program.

The University of Michigan was recognized as having a good adult education service similar to Pennsylvania State University. I referenced Michigan’s program when formulating ideas for Extension to serve alumni and professionals effectively. This helped me convince the President that Extension should not be remedial but aimed at lifelong learning and keeping graduates up to date. Though I was primarily involved with California institutions, I valued these national examples highly.

Stanford University’s motto, 'Die luft die freiheit' or 'The wind of freedom blows,' inspired my approach to Extension. I believed that Extension staff needed freedom and ambition to be effective, much like the spirit embodied by Stanford. Although my main work was with the University of California, Berkeley, I admired Stanford’s philosophy and used it to guide the culture of the Extension program. This motto helped me emphasize the importance of freedom in educational administration during the challenging war years.

entity: College of Engineering, University of California, Berkeley
summary: During World War II, the College of Engineering at the University of California, Berkeley, was assigned to run all courses related to engineering, science, and business administration useful for wartime training. I had been involved in engineering for many years and worked closely with the College to organize these classes. We faced challenges such as recruiting students through companies and ensuring high-level instruction, sometimes involving company vice presidents as teachers. This collaboration was crucial for training personnel for shipyards and other wartime industries.

My work took place during a critical period in the United States, especially during World War II starting in 1942. The country faced shortages of trained engineers and skilled workers, which Extension programs helped address by training both men and women for wartime industries. I was part of a National Advisory Committee appointed by the President of the United States to oversee these efforts. The war created unusual demands and pressures on Extension, requiring coordination with federal agencies and industry to meet national needs.
